preamble.summary: Psalm 119 is the longest chapter in the Bible: 176 verses organized as an alphabetic acrostic of 22 stanzas, one for every letter of the Hebrew alphabet from aleph to tav. Each stanza contains exactly eight verses, and every verse within a stanza begins with that stanza's Hebrew letter. The psalm is a sustained meditation on God's revealed word, and it cycles through eight Hebrew terms for divine revelation: torah (instruction), edut/edot (testimony/testimonies), piqqudim (precepts), chuqqim (statutes), mitsvot (commandments), mishpatim (judgments/ordinances), davar/devarim (word/words), and imrah (saying/promise). Nearly every verse contains at least one of these eight terms. The psalmist is not writing systematic theology but praying through the alphabet — the entire range of human speech — and filling every letter with devotion to God's instruction.
preamble.remarkable: The acrostic structure is not decorative but theological. By writing one stanza for every letter of the Hebrew alphabet, the psalmist says: there is no letter, no sound, no corner of language that cannot be turned toward God's instruction. The alphabet is the raw material of all speech, and the psalmist consecrates every piece of it. The eight rotating torah-words function like a musical theme with variations — each word emphasizes a different facet of revelation (instruction, testimony, precept, statute, command, judgment, word, promise), and their constant recombination across 176 verses creates a kaleidoscopic portrait of what it means to live under God's spoken will. The psalm is also deeply personal: the psalmist weeps, suffers, is persecuted by the arrogant, lies awake at night, and clings to God's word as the one stable reality in a hostile world. This is not a cold catalog of legal terms but an intimate journal of someone who has staked everything on the reliability of what God has said.
preamble.friction: Modern readers sometimes experience Psalm 119 as repetitive, but the repetition is the point. This is a meditation manual — it is designed to be prayed slowly, one stanza at a time, the way a musician practices scales. The eight torah-words are not synonyms but overlapping perspectives on the same reality: torah emphasizes instruction and guidance, edut emphasizes witness and testimony, piqqudim emphasizes specific charges or appointments, chuqqim emphasizes engraved or permanent decrees, mitsvot emphasizes direct commands, mishpatim emphasizes judicial decisions and case law, davar emphasizes the spoken or communicated word, and imrah emphasizes the personal promise or saying. The psalmist's constant petition for understanding (binah, sekhel, da'at) suggests that knowing God's word is not automatic — it requires ongoing illumination.
preamble.connections: Psalm 119 is the climax of the Torah-psalms that begin with Psalm 1 ('Blessed is the one whose delight is in the instruction of the LORD') and Psalm 19 ('The instruction of the LORD is complete, restoring the soul'). It expands the themes of those shorter psalms into a full alphabetic meditation. The eight torah-words echo Deuteronomy's vocabulary for covenant obligation (especially Deuteronomy 4-6). The psalmist's suffering and persecution connect to the lament traditions found throughout the Psalter. The opening beatitude ('Blessed are those whose way is blameless, who walk in the instruction of the LORD') mirrors Psalm 1:1 and frames the entire psalm as a wisdom text.

verses.text_kjv: Blessed are the undefiled in the way, who walk in the law of the LORD.
verses.rendering:
How blessed are those whose way is blameless,
who walk in the instruction of the LORD.
verses.expanded_rendering: The psalm opens with ashrei ('how blessed, how fortunate') — the same word that opens Psalm 1. The connection is deliberate: both psalms declare that the blessed life is defined by orientation toward God's instruction. The phrase temimei darekh ('blameless of way') describes not sinless perfection but wholehearted integrity — tamim means complete, whole, undivided. These are people whose path (derekh) is not fractured by double allegiance. They walk (holkhim) in the torah of the LORD — and torah here is not a legal code but a body of instruction, the full scope of what God has revealed for the ordering of life.
translator_notes: Aleph stanza (vv. 1-8). Every verse in this stanza begins with the Hebrew letter aleph, the first letter of the alphabet. The psalmist begins at the beginning — with the foundational declaration that blessing flows from walking in God's instruction. The word ashrei is a plural construct ('blessings of') functioning as an exclamation — it cannot be reduced to a single English word. 'Happy' is too shallow; 'blessed' risks sounding passive. 'How blessed' preserves the exclamatory force.

key_terms.semantic_range: instruction, teaching, direction, law, guidance, the body of God's revealed will
key_terms.note: Torah is the primary word for God's revelation in this psalm, appearing 25 times. It derives from the verb yarah ('to throw, to cast, to direct, to teach') and fundamentally means direction or instruction — what God has spoken to guide human life. Rendering it as 'law' narrows it to legal command; 'instruction' preserves the broader sense of teaching, guidance, and formation that torah carries throughout the Hebrew Bible.

verses.text_kjv: Blessed are they that keep his testimonies, and that seek him with the whole heart.
verses.rendering:
How blessed are those who guard his testimonies,
who seek him with their whole heart.
translator_notes: The verb natsar ('to guard, to keep watch over, to preserve') implies active vigilance — God's testimonies (edotav) are something to be watched over and protected, not merely obeyed. The word edot ('testimonies') comes from the root ud ('to witness, to testify') and refers to God's declarations that bear witness to his character and will. Seeking (darash) with the whole heart (bekhol lev) means undivided pursuit.

key_terms.semantic_range: testimonies, witness-statements, covenant stipulations, declarations that testify to God's character
key_terms.note: Edot (plural of edut/edah) appears 23 times in this psalm. These are God's self-witnessing declarations — statements that reveal who God is and what God expects. The ark of the covenant was called the aron ha-edut ('ark of the testimony') because it contained God's own witness to the covenant relationship.

verses.text_kjv: They also do no iniquity: they walk in his ways.
verses.rendering:
Indeed they do no wrong;
they walk in his ways.
translator_notes: The word avlah ('injustice, wrong, perversity') describes moral crookedness — the opposite of the blameless (tamim) way described in verse 1. The emphasis is not that these people never fail but that wrongdoing is not their pattern or trajectory. Their habitual path (derekh) is God's path.

verses.text_kjv: Thou hast commanded us to keep thy precepts diligently.
verses.rendering:
You yourself have commanded your precepts
to be kept with care.
translator_notes: The pronoun attah ('you') is emphatic — you yourself, God, are the one who issued these charges. The word piqqudim ('precepts') from the root paqad ('to attend to, to charge, to appoint') refers to specific charges or instructions that God has assigned. The adverb me'od ('very, exceedingly, diligently') intensifies the command: these are not casual suggestions but charges demanding careful attention.

key_terms.semantic_range: precepts, charges, appointed duties, specific instructions, mandates
key_terms.note: Piqqudim appears 21 times in Psalm 119 and is found almost exclusively in this psalm. It derives from paqad ('to visit, to attend to, to charge, to appoint') and conveys the sense of specific, personally assigned duties — God has attended to these matters and charged his people with responsibility for them.

verses.text_kjv: O that my ways were directed to keep thy statutes!
verses.rendering:
If only my ways were firmly set
to keep your statutes!
translator_notes: Achalai ('if only, would that') expresses intense longing — the psalmist recognizes a gap between the blessedness described in verses 1-3 and his own reality. The verb yakonu ('were established, were firmly set') from kun means to be fixed, stable, prepared. The psalmist's ways (derakhay) are not yet firmly directed toward God's chuqqim ('statutes'). This is the psalm's first confession of inadequacy — the meditation begins not with self-congratulation but with desire.

key_terms.semantic_range: statutes, engraved decrees, permanent inscriptions, boundary markers, things carved in stone
key_terms.note: Chuqqim (from chaqaq, 'to engrave, to inscribe, to decree') appears 22 times in this psalm. The root image is carving into stone — these are God's permanent, indelible decrees, written not on paper that can burn but engraved into the bedrock of reality.

verses.text_kjv: Then shall I not be ashamed, when I have respect unto all thy commandments.
verses.rendering:
Then I would not be put to shame
when I look at all your commandments.
translator_notes: The verb bosh ('to be ashamed, to be put to shame') describes the experience of being exposed as inadequate — the psalmist knows that looking honestly at God's mitsvot ('commandments') reveals how far short he falls. Only if his ways were firmly set (v. 5) could he face all of God's commandments without embarrassment.

key_terms.note: Mitsvot (from tsavah, 'to command, to charge, to order') appears 22 times in this psalm. Unlike torah (which emphasizes guidance) or piqqudim (which emphasizes assigned duties), mitsvot emphasizes the authority of the one commanding — these are direct orders from the sovereign.

verses.text_hebrew: א֭וֹדְךָ בְּיֹ֣שֶׁר לֵבָ֑ב בְּ֝לׇמְדִ֗י מִשְׁפְּטֵ֥י צִדְקֶֽךָ׃
verses.text_kjv: I will praise thee with uprightness of heart, when I shall have learned thy righteous judgments.
verses.rendering:
I will give you thanks with an upright heart
as I learn your righteous judgments.
translator_notes: The phrase yosher levav ('uprightness of heart') describes internal integrity — a heart that is straight and undivided. The verb lamad ('to learn') is significant: the psalmist is still learning, still in process. The word mishpetei ('judgments of') from shaphat ('to judge') refers to God's judicial decisions and case rulings — the specific applications of divine justice to real situations.

key_terms.semantic_range: judgments, ordinances, judicial decisions, case law, rulings, justice applied to specific situations
key_terms.note: Mishpatim (from shaphat, 'to judge') appears 23 times in this psalm. These are God's judicial decisions — not abstract principles but specific rulings that establish precedent. In the Torah, the mishpatim section of Exodus 21-23 applies the Ten Commandments to concrete cases.

verses.text_kjv: I will keep thy statutes: O forsake me not utterly.
verses.rendering:
I will keep your statutes —
do not utterly forsake me.
translator_notes: The stanza ends with a pledge and a plea. The psalmist commits to keeping God's statutes (et chuqqekha eshmor), but immediately adds a prayer: al ta'azveni ad me'od ('do not abandon me exceedingly/utterly'). The juxtaposition is revealing — the psalmist knows that his keeping depends on God's not leaving. Obedience is not a solo achievement but a response to divine presence.

verses.text_kjv: Wherewithal shall a young man cleanse his way? by taking heed thereto according to thy word.
verses.rendering:
How can a young person keep his path pure?
By guarding it according to your word.
translator_notes: Bet stanza (vv. 9-16). Every verse begins with the Hebrew letter bet, the second letter of the alphabet. The stanza opens with a practical question: bammeh yezakkeh na'ar et orcho ('by what means can a young person purify his way')? The verb zakhah ('to be pure, to be clean') and the noun na'ar ('young man, youth') together ask how a person at the beginning of life can navigate a straight course. The answer is the davar — God's word.

key_terms.semantic_range: word, speech, matter, thing, event, message, promise, communication
key_terms.note: Davar (plural devarim) appears 24 times in this psalm, making it the most frequent of the eight torah-words. It derives from the verb dabar ('to speak') and refers to God's communicated will — the thing God has said. Unlike torah (which emphasizes instruction) or chuqqim (which emphasizes permanence), davar emphasizes the act of communication: God has spoken, and that speech is the reference point for all of life.

verses.text_kjv: With my whole heart have I sought thee: O let me not wander from thy commandments.
verses.rendering:
With my whole heart I seek you;
do not let me stray from your commandments.
translator_notes: The verb darash ('to seek, to inquire, to pursue') describes active, intentional pursuit — not passive waiting but going after God. The plea al tashgeni ('do not let me stray') uses the Hiphil of shagah ('to wander, to err'), asking God to prevent the wandering that the psalmist knows he is capable of.

verses.text_hebrew: בְּ֭לִבִּי צָפַ֣נְתִּי אִמְרָתֶ֑ךָ לְ֝מַ֗עַן לֹ֣א אֶחֱטָא־לָֽךְ׃
verses.text_kjv: Thy word have I hid in mine heart, that I might not sin against thee.
verses.rendering:
In my heart I have treasured your promise,
so that I will not sin against you.
translator_notes: The verb tsaphan ('to hide, to treasure, to store up') means more than casual memorization — it describes something precious concealed for safekeeping. The word imratekha ('your saying/promise') from the root amar ('to say') refers to God's personal utterance, his spoken promise. This is the eighth and final torah-word to appear in the psalm.

key_terms.semantic_range: saying, utterance, speech, promise, word, command
key_terms.note: Imrah appears 19 times in this psalm. It derives from amar ('to say') and is more personal and intimate than davar — where davar is the communicated word, imrah is the spoken saying, often carrying the nuance of a promise or personal address. The psalmist treasures it in the heart because it is God speaking directly to him.

verses.text_kjv: Blessed art thou, O LORD: teach me thy statutes.
verses.rendering:
Blessed are you, O LORD;
teach me your statutes.
translator_notes: Barukh attah YHWH ('blessed are you, O LORD') is the standard Jewish blessing formula that opens virtually every Hebrew prayer to this day. Here it transitions into petition: lammedeni chuqqekha ('teach me your statutes'). The verb lamad in the Piel ('to teach') asks God to be the instructor.

verses.text_kjv: With my lips have I declared all the judgments of thy mouth.
verses.rendering:
With my lips I recount
all the judgments of your mouth.
translator_notes: The verb sippar ('to recount, to declare, to tell') describes public recitation — the psalmist does not keep God's judgments to himself but speaks them aloud. The phrase mishpetei fikha ('judgments of your mouth') personalizes the source: these judgments come from God's own mouth.

verses.text_kjv: I have rejoiced in the way of thy testimonies, as much as in all riches.
verses.rendering:
In the way of your testimonies I rejoice
as over all wealth.
translator_notes: The verb sas ('to rejoice, to exult') expresses delight — the psalmist finds joy in the path marked out by God's testimonies. The comparison ke'al kol hon ('as over all wealth') is striking: the psalmist's pleasure in God's testimonies equals or exceeds what others feel about riches.

verses.text_kjv: I will meditate in thy precepts, and have respect unto thy ways.
verses.rendering:
On your precepts I will meditate,
and I will fix my gaze on your paths.
translator_notes: The verb siach ('to meditate, to muse, to ponder, to talk to oneself') describes active mental engagement — not passive reading but turning something over in the mind. The verb nabat ('to look at, to gaze upon') in the Hiphil (abbittah) means to fix one's attention deliberately. Both verbs describe focused, sustained attention.

verses.text_kjv: I will delight myself in thy statutes: I will not forget thy word.
verses.rendering:
In your statutes I take delight;
I will not forget your word.
translator_notes: The verb sha'a in the Hitpael (eshtasha) describes luxuriant enjoyment — this is not dutiful compliance but genuine pleasure. The pledge lo eshkach devarekha ('I will not forget your word') closes the stanza with a commitment to memory.

verses.text_kjv: Deal bountifully with thy servant, that I may live, and keep thy word.
verses.rendering:
Deal generously with your servant, that I may live
and keep your word.
translator_notes: Gimel stanza (vv. 17-24). Every verse begins with the Hebrew letter gimel. The stanza opens with a petition: gemol al avdekha ('deal bountifully with your servant'). The verb gamal ('to deal with, to reward, to ripen') asks God to act generously. The purpose clause echeyeh ('that I may live') reveals that the psalmist's very survival depends on God's generous dealing.

verses.text_kjv: Open thou mine eyes, that I may behold wondrous things out of thy law.
verses.rendering:
Uncover my eyes, that I may see
wondrous things from your instruction.
verses.expanded_rendering: The verb galah ('to uncover, to reveal, to roll away') is vivid — gal einai ('uncover my eyes') pictures a veil or covering being removed so that what was hidden becomes visible. The psalmist cannot see the nifla'ot ('wonders, marvelous things') embedded in God's torah by natural sight. The instruction is there, but its depths are hidden until God opens the reader's eyes. This is one of the psalm's most important theological claims: the torah is not self-interpreting. A reader can study it and still miss its wonders. Illumination is a gift, not an achievement.
translator_notes: The word nifla'ot (Niphal participle of pala', 'to be wonderful') refers to things that exceed normal understanding — marvels, wonders. These wonders are described as coming mittorathekha ('from your instruction'), suggesting that the torah contains layers of meaning revealed progressively as God opens the reader's eyes.

verses.text_hebrew: גֵּ֣ר אָנֹכִ֣י בָאָ֑רֶץ אַל־תַּסְתֵּ֥ר מִ֝מֶּ֗נִּי מִצְוֺתֶֽיךָ׃
verses.text_kjv: I am a stranger in the earth: hide not thy commandments from me.
verses.rendering:
I am a stranger in the land;
do not hide your commandments from me.
translator_notes: The word ger ('stranger, sojourner, resident alien') describes someone living in a land that is not their own, without the rights or security of a native. The psalmist's self-identification as a ger suggests that this world is not home — he is passing through and needs God's commandments as a guide for navigation in foreign territory.

verses.text_kjv: My soul breaketh for the longing that it hath unto thy judgments at all times.
verses.rendering:
My soul is crushed with longing
for your judgments at all times.
translator_notes: The verb garas ('to crush, to break, to grind') describes physical grinding or crushing — the psalmist's soul (nephesh) is being ground to pieces by the intensity of its longing (ta'avah) for God's judgments. This is not mild interest but consuming desire. The phrase bekhol et ('at all times') means this longing never relents.

verses.text_kjv: Thou hast rebuked the proud that are cursed, which do err from thy commandments.
verses.rendering:
You rebuke the arrogant — they are cursed,
those who stray from your commandments.
translator_notes: The verb ga'ar ('to rebuke, to blast, to threaten') describes God's forceful reprimand. The zedim ('arrogant, presumptuous, proud') are a recurring enemy throughout this psalm — those who willfully disregard God's commands. They are arurim ('cursed') — the opposite of the ashrei ('blessed') in verse 1.

verses.text_hebrew: גַּ֣ל מֵ֭עָלַי חֶרְפָּ֣ה וָב֑וּז כִּ֖י עֵדֹתֶ֣יךָ נָצָֽרְתִּי׃
verses.text_kjv: Remove from me reproach and contempt; for I have kept thy testimonies.
verses.rendering:
Roll away from me scorn and contempt,
for I have guarded your testimonies.
translator_notes: The verb galal ('to roll') in the imperative (gal) pictures shame as a heavy stone that the psalmist wants God to roll off of him. The words cherpah ('reproach, scorn') and buz ('contempt') suggest that the psalmist is being mocked for his devotion to God's testimonies.

verses.text_hebrew: גַּ֤ם יָשְׁב֣וּ שָׂ֭רִים בִּ֣י נִדְבָּ֑רוּ עַ֝בְדְּךָ֗ יָשִׂ֥יחַ בְּחֻקֶּֽיךָ׃
verses.text_kjv: Princes also did sit and speak against me: but thy servant did meditate in thy statutes.
verses.rendering:
Even when rulers sit and conspire against me,
your servant meditates on your statutes.
translator_notes: The sarim ('rulers, princes, officials') who conspire against the psalmist represent the highest level of opposition — this is not casual mockery but organized, powerful hostility. Against this political threat, the psalmist's response is meditation (yasiach) on God's statutes — quiet, sustained attention to divine truth in the face of human power.

verses.text_kjv: Thy testimonies also are my delight and my counsellors.
verses.rendering:
Yes, your testimonies are my delight,
my counselors.
translator_notes: The word sha'ashu'ai ('my delights') from sha'a describes luxuriant, overflowing pleasure. The phrase anshei atsati ('men of my counsel') personifies the testimonies as trusted advisors — where rulers plot against the psalmist (v. 23), God's testimonies serve as his inner circle of counsel.

verses.text_kjv: My soul cleaveth unto the dust: quicken thou me according to thy word.
verses.rendering:
My soul clings to the dust;
revive me according to your word.
verses.expanded_rendering: The verb davaq ('to cling, to adhere, to stick') is the same verb used in Genesis 2:24 for a man clinging to his wife — it describes an intimate, inseparable bond. But here the soul clings to dust (afar), the substance of death and return (Genesis 3:19). The psalmist is pressed flat against the ground, whether by grief, persecution, or spiritual exhaustion. The petition chayyeni ('revive me, give me life') from the root chayah ('to live') is one of the psalm's most frequent prayers — it will appear eleven times. The psalmist does not merely need encouragement; he needs life itself, and he asks for it kidvarekha ('according to your word') — on the basis of what God has promised.
translator_notes: Dalet stanza (vv. 25-32). Every verse begins with the Hebrew letter dalet. This stanza is marked by suffering and urgent petition — the psalmist is in the dust, weeping, melting with grief, and begging God for revival.

verses.text_kjv: I have declared my ways, and thou heardest me: teach me thy statutes.
verses.rendering:
I told you my ways and you answered me;
teach me your statutes.
translator_notes: The verb sippar ('to recount, to tell') here describes the psalmist opening his life to God — derakhay sipparti ('my ways I recounted'). This is confession: laying out one's path before God. And God answered (vatta'aneni). Now the psalmist asks for the next step: lammedeni chuqqekha. The sequence is important: confession, divine response, request for further instruction.

verses.text_kjv: Make me to understand the way of thy precepts: so shall I talk of thy wondrous works.
verses.rendering:
Make me understand the way of your precepts,
and I will meditate on your wonders.
translator_notes: The verb havineni ('make me understand') from bin ('to discern') in the Hiphil is a plea for insight — not just knowledge but comprehension of how the precepts connect and apply. Understanding the precepts opens the door to seeing the wonders (nifle'otekha) embedded in them.

verses.text_kjv: My soul melteth for heaviness: strengthen thou me according unto thy word.
verses.rendering:
My soul melts with grief;
raise me up according to your word.
translator_notes: The verb dalaf ('to drip, to weep, to dissolve') describes the soul literally dissolving — dalefah nafshi mittugah ('my soul drips/melts from sorrow'). The image is of a person so grief-stricken that they are liquefying. The petition qayyemeni ('raise me up, establish me') asks God to give back the solidity that grief has dissolved.

verses.text_kjv: Remove from me the way of lying: and grant me thy law graciously.
verses.rendering:
Remove from me the way of falsehood,
and graciously grant me your instruction.
translator_notes: The derekh sheqer ('way of falsehood') is the opposite of the derekh emunah ('way of faithfulness') in verse 30. The psalmist asks God to remove it — haser ('take away') — suggesting that the pull toward falsehood cannot be resisted by willpower alone. The petition vetoratkha chonneni ('and your instruction graciously grant me') uses chanan ('to be gracious') — the granting of torah is an act of grace.

verses.text_kjv: I have chosen the way of truth: thy judgments have I laid before me.
verses.rendering:
I have chosen the way of faithfulness;
your judgments I have set before me.
translator_notes: The derekh emunah ('way of faithfulness') is the psalmist's chosen path — bacharti ('I have chosen') is a deliberate, conscious decision. The word emunah means faithfulness, reliability, steadfastness. The verb shivviti ('I have set, I have placed') means the psalmist has positioned God's judgments directly in front of himself as a constant reference point.

verses.text_kjv: I have stuck unto thy testimonies: O LORD, put me not to shame.
verses.rendering:
I cling to your testimonies, O LORD;
do not put me to shame.
translator_notes: The verb davaq ('to cling') reappears from verse 25, but now the clinging has shifted: where the soul clung to dust (despair), the psalmist now clings to God's testimonies. The petition al tevisheni ('do not put me to shame') echoes verse 6 — shame is the constant fear of one who has staked everything on God's faithfulness.

verses.text_kjv: I will run the way of thy commandments, when thou shalt enlarge my heart.
verses.rendering:
I will run the way of your commandments,
for you enlarge my heart.
verses.expanded_rendering: The verb ruts ('to run') is striking — the psalmist does not merely walk the way of God's commandments but runs it, with eagerness and energy. The reason for this running is ki tarchiv libbi ('for you enlarge my heart'). The verb rachav ('to be wide, to be broad, to enlarge') pictures the heart being expanded, opened up, given more capacity. When God enlarges the heart, obedience becomes not a narrow, constricted duty but a wide, open-field run. The psalmist is saying: obedience feels like freedom when God expands the inner capacity for it. This verse is the theological hinge of the Dalet stanza: the psalmist who was melting in grief (v. 28) and clinging to dust (v. 25) now runs — because God has made room inside him.
translator_notes: The image of an enlarged heart (lev rachav) is the opposite of the narrow, constricted heart of anxiety and fear. When the heart is wide, it can contain more understanding, more desire, more joy, more obedience. The running is the result of the enlarging, not its cause.

verses.text_hebrew: הוֹרֵ֣נִי יְ֭הוָה דֶּ֥רֶךְ חֻקֶּ֗יךָ וְאֶצְּרֶ֥נָּה עֵֽקֶב׃
verses.text_kjv: Teach me, O LORD, the way of thy statutes; and I shall keep it unto the end.
verses.rendering:
Teach me, O LORD, the way of your statutes,
and I will keep it to the end.
translator_notes: He stanza (vv. 33-40). Every verse begins with the Hebrew letter he. This stanza is dominated by petitions — the psalmist asks God to teach, give understanding, lead, turn the heart, turn the eyes, confirm, and take away. The verb horeni ('teach me') from yarah (the same root as torah) asks God to be the one who points the way. The word eqev ('end, consequence') suggests perseverance to the finish.

verses.text_kjv: Give me understanding, and I shall keep thy law: yea, I shall observe it with my whole heart.
verses.rendering:
Give me understanding, and I will guard your instruction
and keep it with my whole heart.
translator_notes: Again the verb havineni ('give me understanding') — the Hiphil imperative of bin. Understanding is not self-generated but received from God, and when received, it produces wholehearted obedience.

verses.text_kjv: Make me to go in the path of thy commandments; for therein do I delight.
verses.rendering:
Lead me in the path of your commandments,
for in it I delight.
translator_notes: The verb hadrikheni ('lead me, cause me to tread') from darakh in the Hiphil asks God to actively guide the psalmist's steps. The word nativ ('path, pathway') describes a narrow, well-worn trail. The motivation is delight: ki vo chafatsti ('for in it I take pleasure').

verses.text_kjv: Incline my heart unto thy testimonies, and not to covetousness.
verses.rendering:
Turn my heart toward your testimonies
and not toward selfish gain.
translator_notes: The verb natah ('to stretch out, to incline, to turn') in the imperative asks God to tilt the psalmist's heart in the right direction. The heart can be inclined toward God's testimonies or toward batsa ('unjust gain, selfish profit, greed'). The psalmist does not trust his own heart's direction and asks God to set its angle.

verses.text_hebrew: הַעֲבֵ֣ר עֵ֭ינַי מֵרְא֣וֹת שָׁ֑וְא בִּדְרָכֶ֥ךָ חַיֵּֽנִי׃
verses.text_kjv: Turn away mine eyes from beholding vanity; and quicken thou me in thy way.
verses.rendering:
Turn my eyes away from looking at worthless things;
revive me in your way.
translator_notes: The verb ha'aver ('cause to pass, turn away') asks God to redirect the psalmist's gaze. The word shav ('emptiness, vanity, worthlessness') describes things that have no substance — distractions that pull the eyes away from what matters. The petition chayyeni ('revive me') returns, now linked to bidrekhekha ('in your way').

verses.text_kjv: Stablish thy word unto thy servant, who is devoted to thy fear.
verses.rendering:
Confirm your promise to your servant,
which leads to fearing you.
translator_notes: The verb haqem ('establish, confirm') asks God to make his promise (imrah) firm in the psalmist's experience. The phrase asher leyir'atekha ('which leads to your fear') connects the confirmed promise to the fear of the LORD — when God keeps his word, the natural response is reverent awe.

verses.text_hebrew: הַעֲבֵ֣ר חֶ֭רְפָּתִי אֲשֶׁ֣ר יָגֹ֑רְתִּי כִּ֖י מִשְׁפָּטֶ֣יךָ טוֹבִֽים׃
verses.text_kjv: Turn away my reproach which I fear: for thy judgments are good.
verses.rendering:
Take away the disgrace I dread,
for your judgments are good.
translator_notes: The verb ha'aver ('take away') reappears from verse 37. The verb yagor ('to fear, to dread') reveals anxiety about being publicly shamed. The ground of his appeal is that God's judgments are tovim ('good') — if God's rulings are good, then those who follow them should not be disgraced.

verses.text_kjv: Behold, I have longed after thy precepts: quicken me in thy righteousness.
verses.rendering:
See how I long for your precepts!
Revive me in your righteousness.
translator_notes: The interjection hinneh ('see! behold!') draws God's attention to the psalmist's longing. The verb ta'av ('to long for, to desire') describes deep yearning for God's precepts. The petition chayyeni ('revive me') now rests on betsidqatekha ('in your righteousness') — God's own righteous character is the basis for the request.

verses.text_kjv: Let thy mercies come also unto me, O LORD, even thy salvation, according to thy word.
verses.rendering:
Let your faithful love come to me, O LORD —
your salvation, according to your promise.
translator_notes: Vav stanza (vv. 41-48). Every verse begins with the Hebrew letter vav. The stanza opens with a petition for God's chesed ('faithful love') — the covenant loyalty that defines God's commitment to his people. The word teshu'atekha ('your salvation') parallels the faithful love: salvation is the concrete expression of chesed.

verses.text_kjv: So shall I have wherewith to answer him that reproacheth me: for I trust in thy word.
verses.rendering:
Then I will have an answer for those who taunt me,
for I trust in your word.
translator_notes: The psalmist needs an answer (e'eneh) for his mockers (chorfi, 'those who reproach me'). The answer is not a clever argument but a reality: if God's faithful love comes (v. 41), the psalmist will have something to point to. The basis of his confidence is batachti bidvarekha ('I trust in your word').

verses.text_hebrew: וְאַל־תַּצֵּ֬ל מִפִּ֣י דְבַר־אֱמֶ֣ת עַד־מְאֹ֑ד כִּ֖י לְמִשְׁפָּטֶ֣ךָ יִחָֽלְתִּי׃
verses.text_kjv: And take not the word of truth utterly out of my mouth; for I have hoped in thy judgments.
verses.rendering:
Do not snatch the word of truth from my mouth entirely,
for I put my hope in your judgments.
translator_notes: The verb natsal ('to snatch away') in the Hiphil here means to take away forcefully. The psalmist fears losing the devar emet ('word of truth') — the ability to speak truth. The verb yachal ('to wait, to hope') describes patient expectation directed at God's judgments.

verses.text_kjv: So shall I keep thy law continually for ever and ever.
verses.rendering:
I will keep your instruction continually,
forever and ever.
translator_notes: The phrase tamid le'olam va'ed ('continually, forever and ever') stacks three time expressions for emphasis. The psalmist's commitment to keeping Torah is not temporary but lifelong and, in his aspiration, eternal.

verses.text_kjv: And I will walk at liberty: for I seek thy precepts.
verses.rendering:
I will walk in a wide open place,
for I have sought your precepts.
verses.expanded_rendering: The word rechavah ('wide open space, broad place, liberty') pictures freedom as spaciousness — the opposite of constriction. The psalmist walks in this openness because he has sought (darashti) God's precepts. This is a profound paradox: the person who submits to God's detailed instructions experiences not confinement but expansion. The precepts do not narrow life; they open it into a broad, free space. This echoes verse 32 where God enlarges the heart — the internal expansion there corresponds to the external spaciousness here.
translator_notes: The word rechavah is related to rachav ('wide, broad') from verse 32. The psalmist's experience of freedom is directly connected to his engagement with God's precepts — obedience produces liberty, not bondage.

verses.text_hebrew: וַאֲדַבְּרָ֣ה בְ֭עֵדֹתֶיךָ נֶ֥גֶד מְלָכִ֗ים וְלֹ֣א אֵבֽוֹשׁ׃
verses.text_kjv: I will speak of thy testimonies also before kings, and will not be ashamed.
verses.rendering:
I will speak of your testimonies before kings
and will not be ashamed.
translator_notes: The psalmist envisions speaking (adabberah) of God's testimonies in the presence of melakhim ('kings'). The phrase velo evosh ('and I will not be ashamed') reveals courage rooted in conviction. Where rulers conspired against the psalmist in verse 23, here the psalmist speaks before kings without shame.

verses.text_kjv: And I will delight myself in thy commandments, which I have loved.
verses.rendering:
I will delight in your commandments,
which I love.
translator_notes: The verb sha'a in the Hitpael (eshtasha) again describes luxuriant delight. The addition of asher ahavti ('which I love') makes the emotion explicit: delight and love together describe the psalmist's relationship to God's commandments.

verses.text_hebrew: וְאֶשָּׂ֣א כַ֭פַּי אֶל־מִצְוֺתֶ֣יךָ אֲשֶׁ֣ר אָהָ֑בְתִּי וְ֝אָשִׂ֗יחָה בְּחֻקֶּֽיךָ׃
verses.text_kjv: My hands also will I lift up unto thy commandments, which I have loved; and I will meditate in thy statutes.
verses.rendering:
I will lift my hands toward your commandments, which I love,
and I will meditate on your statutes.
translator_notes: Lifting hands (essa khappai) is a gesture of prayer, worship, and reaching — the psalmist stretches toward God's commandments as a worshiper stretches toward God. The stanza closes with meditation (asichah) on statutes.

verses.text_kjv: Remember the word unto thy servant, upon which thou hast caused me to hope.
verses.rendering:
Remember your word to your servant,
in which you have given me hope.
translator_notes: Zayin stanza (vv. 49-56). Every verse begins with the Hebrew letter zayin. The stanza opens with zekhor ('remember') — an imperative addressed to God. The psalmist is not reminding a forgetful God but calling on God to act on what he has already said.

verses.text_hebrew: זֹ֣את נֶ֭חָמָתִי בְעׇנְיִ֑י כִּ֖י אִמְרָתְךָ֣ חִיָּֽתְנִי׃
verses.text_kjv: This is my comfort in my affliction: for thy word hath quickened me.
verses.rendering:
This is my comfort in my suffering:
that your promise has revived me.
translator_notes: The word nechamati ('my comfort') from nacham identifies what sustains the psalmist through onyi ('suffering, affliction'). The source: ki imratekha chiyyatni ('for your promise has revived me'). God's spoken promise has actual life-giving power.

verses.text_hebrew: זֵ֭דִים הֱלִיצֻ֣נִי עַד־מְאֹ֑ד מִ֝תּוֹרָתְךָ֗ לֹ֣א נָטִֽיתִי׃
verses.text_kjv: The proud have had me greatly in derision: yet have I not declined from thy law.
verses.rendering:
The arrogant mock me bitterly,
but I do not turn from your instruction.
translator_notes: The zedim ('arrogant') reappear from verse 21 — persistent antagonists throughout the psalm. The verb luts ('to mock, to scorn') in the Hiphil describes withering ridicule. Despite intense mockery (ad me'od), the psalmist has not turned aside (lo natiti) from God's torah.

verses.text_kjv: I remembered thy judgments of old, O LORD; and have comforted myself.
verses.rendering:
I remember your judgments from of old, O LORD,
and I find comfort.
translator_notes: The phrase me'olam ('from of old') points to God's historical track record of just rulings. Remembering God's past judgments produces comfort (va'etnecham) in the present. In verse 49 the psalmist asked God to remember; here he himself remembers.

verses.text_kjv: Horror hath taken hold upon me because of the wicked that forsake thy law.
verses.rendering:
Burning indignation seizes me
because of the wicked who abandon your instruction.
translator_notes: The word zal'afah ('burning heat, indignation') describes an intense emotional reaction — the psalmist is physically seized (achazatni) by scorching anger at the wicked's abandonment of Torah. The reshaim ('wicked') are defined as ozvei toratekha ('those who abandon your instruction').

verses.text_kjv: Thy statutes have been my songs in the house of my pilgrimage.
verses.rendering:
Your statutes have been my songs
in the house of my sojourning.
verses.expanded_rendering: The word zemirot ('songs, psalms') transforms statutes into music — God's chuqqim are not merely studied but sung. The phrase bevet meguray ('in the house of my sojourning') connects to the psalmist's self-identification as a ger ('stranger') in verse 19. The sojourning house is wherever the psalmist lives during his earthly pilgrimage, and in that temporary dwelling, God's statutes provide not just guidance but music — they are what the psalmist sings to himself during the journey. This verse is one of the psalm's most beautiful: the heavy, engraved decrees of God become light enough to carry as a song.
translator_notes: The word meguray ('my sojourning') from gur ('to sojourn') reinforces the theme from verse 19: the psalmist is a traveler, and God's statutes are his traveling songs.

verses.text_hebrew: זָ֘כַ֤רְתִּי בַלַּ֣יְלָה שִׁמְךָ֣ יְהוָ֑ה וָ֝אֶשְׁמְרָ֗ה תּוֹרָתֶֽךָ׃
verses.text_kjv: I have remembered thy name, O LORD, in the night, and have kept thy law.
verses.rendering:
I remember your name in the night, O LORD,
and I keep your instruction.
translator_notes: The nighttime remembrance (zakharti ballailah) suggests lying awake — the psalmist's waking hours in the dark are spent remembering God's name (shimkha). The name is not just a label but the full character and identity of God.

verses.text_kjv: This I had, because I kept thy precepts.
verses.rendering:
This has been my way of life:
that I have guarded your precepts.
translator_notes: The demonstrative zo't ('this') sums up everything described in the stanza — the comfort, the songs, the nighttime remembrance — and attributes it all to guarding God's precepts.

verses.text_kjv: Thou art my portion, O LORD: I have said that I would keep thy words.
verses.rendering:
The LORD is my portion;
I have promised to keep your words.
verses.expanded_rendering: The word chelqi ('my portion, my share, my inheritance') is a land-distribution term — when Israel divided Canaan, each tribe received its cheleq. The Levites received no land because 'the LORD is their portion' (Deuteronomy 10:9). The psalmist claims the same: God himself, not land or wealth, is the inheritance. This declaration opens the Chet stanza with a radical reorientation of value — everything the psalmist needs is found in the LORD, and the response is to keep God's words.
translator_notes: Chet stanza (vv. 57-64). Every verse begins with the Hebrew letter chet. The stanza moves from declaration of God as the psalmist's portion to urgent petition for grace, and it ends with one of the psalm's warmest statements about community.

verses.text_kjv: I intreated thy favour with my whole heart: be merciful unto me according to thy word.
verses.rendering:
I seek your favor with my whole heart;
be gracious to me according to your promise.
translator_notes: The verb chillah ('to entreat, to seek favor') literally means to make someone's face smooth or pleasant — to seek favorable attention. The petition chonneni ('be gracious to me') from chanan asks for unmerited favor based on God's own promise.

verses.text_kjv: I thought on my ways, and turned my feet unto thy testimonies.
verses.rendering:
I considered my ways
and turned my feet back to your testimonies.
translator_notes: The verb chishav ('to think, to reckon, to consider') describes careful self-examination. The result of honest reflection is turning (va'ashivah, 'I caused to return') his feet toward God's testimonies. Self-examination leads to course correction.

verses.text_kjv: I made haste, and delayed not to keep thy commandments.
verses.rendering:
I hurried and did not delay
to keep your commandments.
translator_notes: The verb chush ('to hurry') paired with lo hitmahemahti ('I did not linger') creates urgency — the psalmist did not procrastinate in his return to obedience.

verses.text_hebrew: חֶבְלֵ֣י רְ֭שָׁעִים עִוְּדֻ֑נִי תּ֝וֹרָתְךָ֗ לֹ֣א שָׁכָֽחְתִּי׃
verses.text_kjv: The bands of the wicked have robbed me: but I have not forgotten thy law.
verses.rendering:
The ropes of the wicked have ensnared me,
but I have not forgotten your instruction.
translator_notes: The word chevlei ('ropes, cords, snares') from chevel describes being bound or trapped. Despite being physically ensnared, the psalmist's mind remains free: toratekha lo shakhachti ('your instruction I have not forgotten'). The body may be bound, but the memory of Torah cannot be taken.

verses.text_hebrew: חֲצוֹת־לַ֭יְלָה אָ֭קוּם לְהוֹד֣וֹת לָ֑ךְ עַ֝֗ל מִשְׁפְּטֵ֥י צִדְקֶֽךָ׃
verses.text_kjv: At midnight I will rise to give thanks unto thee because of thy righteous judgments.
verses.rendering:
At midnight I rise to give you thanks
for your righteous judgments.
translator_notes: Chatsot lailah ('midnight') is the darkest hour — and the psalmist rises (aqum) not from fear but from gratitude. The midnight thanksgiving for mishpetei tsidqekha ('your righteous judgments') suggests that even in the darkest circumstances, the psalmist finds reason to praise God's justice.

verses.text_hebrew: חָבֵ֣ר אָ֭נִי לְכׇל־אֲשֶׁ֣ר יְרֵא֑וּךָ וּ֝לְשֹׁמְרֵ֗י פִּקּוּדֶֽיךָ׃
verses.text_kjv: I am a companion of all them that fear thee, and of them that keep thy precepts.
verses.rendering:
I am a companion of all who fear you,
of those who keep your precepts.
translator_notes: The word chaver ('companion, friend') describes a bond based on shared commitment. The psalmist's community is defined not by tribe or geography but by shared reverence for God and shared obedience to his precepts.

verses.text_hebrew: חַסְדְּךָ֣ יְ֭הוָה מָלְאָ֥ה הָאָ֗רֶץ חֻקֶּ֥יךָ לַמְּדֵֽנִי׃
verses.text_kjv: The earth, O LORD, is full of thy mercy: teach me thy statutes.
verses.rendering:
The earth is full of your faithful love, O LORD;
teach me your statutes.
translator_notes: The declaration chasdekha YHWH male'ah ha'arets ('your faithful love fills the earth') is a cosmic statement — God's chesed saturates the entire created order. From this abundance, the psalmist draws a specific request: teach me your statutes.

verses.text_kjv: Thou hast dealt well with thy servant, O LORD, according unto thy word.
verses.rendering:
You have dealt well with your servant,
O LORD, according to your word.
translator_notes: Tet stanza (vv. 65-72). Every verse begins with the Hebrew letter tet. The word tov ('good') is the stanza's theme — the psalmist will use it seven times in eight verses. The opening declaration tov asita ('good you have done') is a retrospective acknowledgment: God has kept his word, and the result has been good.

verses.text_hebrew: ט֤וּב טַ֣עַם וָדַ֣עַת לַמְּדֵ֑נִי כִּ֖י בְמִצְוֺתֶ֣יךָ הֶאֱמָֽנְתִּי׃
verses.text_kjv: Teach me good judgment and knowledge: for I have believed thy commandments.
verses.rendering:
Teach me good judgment and knowledge,
for I trust in your commandments.
translator_notes: The phrase tuv ta'am vada'at ('good discernment and knowledge') combines two faculties: ta'am ('taste, discernment, judgment') and da'at ('knowledge, awareness'). The psalmist wants both: the capacity to know and the capacity to judge well.

verses.text_hebrew: טֶ֣רֶם אֶ֭עֱנֶה אֲנִ֣י שֹׁגֵ֑ג וְ֝עַתָּ֗ה אִמְרָתְךָ֥ שָׁמָֽרְתִּי׃
verses.text_kjv: Before I was afflicted I went astray: but now have I kept thy word.
verses.rendering:
Before I was humbled I went astray,
but now I keep your promise.
verses.expanded_rendering: The verb anah ('to be humbled, to be afflicted') and the verb shagag ('to go astray, to wander unintentionally') frame a before-and-after story. Before suffering came, the psalmist wandered — the straying was not willful rebellion but going off-course without realizing it. Affliction redirected him. Now (ve'attah) he keeps God's promise. This is one of the psalm's most honest confessions: suffering served as a corrective. The psalmist does not celebrate suffering for its own sake but acknowledges that it accomplished what comfort did not — it brought him back to God's word.
translator_notes: The verb anah in the Niphal ('I was humbled/afflicted') is the same root used for the Day of Atonement fasting (Leviticus 16:29). The suffering described is formative — it changed the psalmist's trajectory.

verses.text_kjv: Thou art good, and doest good; teach me thy statutes.
verses.rendering:
You are good and you do good;
teach me your statutes.
translator_notes: The declaration tov attah umetiv ('you are good and you do good') is theologically foundational — God's character (good) and God's actions (does good) are perfectly aligned. If God is good and does good, then teaching his statutes is consistent with his nature.

verses.text_hebrew: טָפְל֬וּ עָלַ֣י שֶׁ֣קֶר זֵדִ֑ים אֲ֝נִ֗י בְּכׇל־לֵ֤ב ׀ אֶצֹּ֬ר פִּקּוּדֶֽיךָ׃
verses.text_kjv: The proud have forged a lie against me: but I will keep thy precepts with my whole heart.
verses.rendering:
The arrogant smear me with lies,
but I guard your precepts with my whole heart.
translator_notes: The verb taphal ('to smear, to plaster, to fabricate') describes the arrogant coating the psalmist with falsehood — literally plastering lies onto him. Against this external fabrication, the psalmist opposes internal wholeness: bekhol lev etsor piqqudekha.

verses.text_hebrew: טָפַ֣שׁ כַּ֭חֵלֶב לִבָּ֑ם אֲ֝נִ֗י תּוֹרָתְךָ֥ שִׁעֲשָֽׁעְתִּי׃
verses.text_kjv: Their heart is as fat as grease; but I delight in thy law.
verses.rendering:
Their heart is thick like fat,
but I delight in your instruction.
translator_notes: The verb tapash ('to be gross, to be insensitive') describes the enemies' hearts as chelev ('fat') — insensitive, unable to feel or perceive. A fat heart is a numb heart. Where their heart is deadened, the psalmist's heart is alive with delight (shi'asha'ti) in Torah.

verses.text_kjv: It is good for me that I have been afflicted; that I might learn thy statutes.
verses.rendering:
It was good for me that I was humbled,
so that I might learn your statutes.
translator_notes: This verse completes the thought from verse 67: suffering was pedagogical — it taught what comfort could not teach. The psalmist does not glorify suffering but acknowledges its instructive function.

verses.text_kjv: The law of thy mouth is better unto me than thousands of gold and silver.
verses.rendering:
The instruction from your mouth is better to me
than thousands of gold and silver pieces.
translator_notes: The phrase torat pikha ('the instruction of your mouth') personalizes the torah — it comes from God's own mouth, not from an impersonal legal code. The comparison me'alfei zahav vakhasef ('than thousands of gold and silver') echoes verse 14's comparison to wealth.

verses.text_hebrew: יָדֶ֣יךָ עָ֭שׂוּנִי וַיְכוֹנְנ֑וּנִי הֲ֝בִינֵ֗נִי וְאֶלְמְדָ֥ה מִצְוֺתֶֽיךָ׃
verses.text_kjv: Thy hands have made me and fashioned me: give me understanding, that I may learn thy commandments.
verses.rendering:
Your hands made me and formed me;
give me understanding, that I may learn your commandments.
translator_notes: Yod stanza (vv. 73-80). Every verse begins with the Hebrew letter yod. The stanza opens with a creation claim: yadekha asuni vayekhonnenuni ('your hands made me and established me'). From this creation comes a logical request: the God who made the psalmist should give him understanding to learn God's commandments.

verses.text_hebrew: יְרֵאֶ֥יךָ יִרְא֑וּנִי וְ֝יִשְׂמָ֗חוּ כִּ֣י לִדְבָרְךָ֥ יִחָֽלְתִּי׃
verses.text_kjv: They that fear thee will be glad when they see me; because I have hoped in thy word.
verses.rendering:
Those who fear you see me and rejoice,
for I have put my hope in your word.
translator_notes: The yere'ekha ('those who fear you') form the psalmist's community. They rejoice when they see him because his hope in God's word is a visible testimony — his perseverance through suffering encourages others who share his reverence.

verses.text_hebrew: יָדַ֣עְתִּי יְ֭הוָה כִּי־צֶ֣דֶק מִשְׁפָּטֶ֑יךָ וֶ֝אֱמוּנָ֗ה עִנִּיתָֽנִי׃
verses.text_kjv: I know, O LORD, that thy judgments are right, and that thou in faithfulness hast afflicted me.
verses.rendering:
I know, O LORD, that your judgments are righteous
and that in faithfulness you humbled me.
translator_notes: The claim ve'emunah innitani ('in faithfulness you afflicted me') is extraordinary — the suffering was not a failure of God's love but an expression of it. This is the same emunah that defines God's covenant character (Exodus 34:6), now applied to the experience of pain.

verses.text_kjv: Let, I pray thee, thy merciful kindness be for my comfort, according to thy word unto thy servant.
verses.rendering:
Let your faithful love be my comfort,
according to your promise to your servant.
translator_notes: The combination of chesed ('faithful love') and imrah ('promise') creates a double foundation: God's character (faithful love) and God's speech (his promise). Comfort rests on both who God is and what God has said.

verses.text_kjv: Let thy tender mercies come unto me, that I may live: for thy law is my delight.
verses.rendering:
Let your compassion come to me, that I may live,
for your instruction is my delight.
translator_notes: The word rachamekha ('your compassion') from rechem ('womb') carries visceral warmth. The psalmist needs this compassion to live (ve'echyeh) — without it, death is the alternative.

verses.text_hebrew: יֵבֹ֣שׁוּ זֵ֭דִים כִּי־שֶׁ֣קֶר עִוְּת֑וּנִי אֲ֝נִ֗י אָשִׂ֥יחַ בְּפִקּוּדֶֽיךָ׃
verses.text_kjv: Let the proud be ashamed; for they dealt perversely with me without a cause: but I will meditate in thy precepts.
verses.rendering:
Let the arrogant be put to shame, for they wrong me with lies;
but I — I will meditate on your precepts.
translator_notes: The psalmist asks that the zedim receive the shame they tried to inflict on him. His response is not retaliation but meditation (asiach) on God's precepts.

verses.text_kjv: Let those that fear thee turn unto me, and those that have known thy testimonies.
verses.rendering:
Let those who fear you turn to me,
those who know your testimonies.
translator_notes: The verb yashuvu ('let them return') asks for the restoration of community. Persecution may have isolated the psalmist, and he asks God to rebuild his fellowship with the faithful.

verses.text_hebrew: יְהִי־לִבִּ֣י תָ֭מִים בְּחֻקֶּ֑יךָ לְ֝מַ֗עַן לֹ֣א אֵבֽוֹשׁ׃
verses.text_kjv: Let my heart be sound in thy statutes; that I be not ashamed.
verses.rendering:
Let my heart be blameless in your statutes,
so that I will not be put to shame.
translator_notes: The prayer yehi libbi tamim ('let my heart be blameless/complete') uses the same word tamim from verse 1 — the psalmist wants the wholeness he praised in others to become his own reality.

verses.text_kjv: My soul fainteth for thy salvation: but I hope in thy word.
verses.rendering:
My soul wastes away for your salvation;
I put my hope in your word.
translator_notes: Kaph stanza (vv. 81-88). Every verse begins with the Hebrew letter kaph. This is one of the psalm's darkest stanzas — the psalmist is exhausted, eyes failing, skin shriveled, nearly destroyed. The verb kalah ('to be finished, to be consumed') describes the soul reaching its limit.

verses.text_hebrew: כָּל֣וּ עֵ֭ינַי לְאִמְרָתֶ֑ךָ לֵ֝אמֹ֗ר מָתַ֥י תְּנַחֲמֵֽנִי׃
verses.text_kjv: Mine eyes fail for thy word, saying, When wilt thou comfort me?
verses.rendering:
My eyes fail with watching for your promise,
saying, 'When will you comfort me?'
translator_notes: The eyes fail (khalu) from straining to see the fulfillment of God's promise. The question matai tenachameni ('when will you comfort me?') is one of the psalm's rawest cries — it does not question whether God will comfort but when.

verses.text_hebrew: כִּי־הָ֭יִיתִי כְּנֹ֣אד בְּקִיט֑וֹר חֻ֝קֶּ֗יךָ לֹ֣א שָׁכָֽחְתִּי׃
verses.text_kjv: For I am become like a bottle in the smoke; yet do I not forget thy statutes.
verses.rendering:
Though I have become like a wineskin in smoke,
I do not forget your statutes.
translator_notes: A wineskin (no'd) hung in smoke (qitor) shrivels, darkens, and cracks — it becomes useless and unrecognizable. The psalmist uses this image for his own condition: dried out, shrunken, damaged. Yet even in this shriveled state, he has not forgotten God's statutes.

verses.text_hebrew: כַּמָּ֥ה יְמֵ֣י עַבְדֶּ֑ךָ מָתַ֬י תַּעֲשֶׂ֖ה בְרֹדְפַ֣י מִשְׁפָּֽט׃
verses.text_kjv: How many are the days of thy servant? when wilt thou execute judgment on them that persecute me?
verses.rendering:
How many days must your servant endure?
When will you execute judgment on my pursuers?
translator_notes: The question kammah yemei avdekha ('how many are the days of your servant?') is a plea about the shortness and weariness of life. The psalmist wants God to act while there is still life left.

verses.text_hebrew: כָּרוּ־לִ֣י זֵ֭דִים שִׁיח֑וֹת אֲשֶׁ֖ר לֹ֣א כְתוֹרָתֶֽךָ׃
verses.text_kjv: The proud have digged pits for me, which are not after thy law.
verses.rendering:
The arrogant have dug pits for me —
they who do not follow your instruction.
translator_notes: The verb karah ('to dig') describes the zedim setting traps — shichot ('pits') dug in the psalmist's path. The relative clause defines the arrogant by their disregard for Torah.

verses.text_kjv: All thy commandments are faithful: they persecute me wrongfully; help thou me.
verses.rendering:
All your commandments are faithful;
they persecute me with lies — help me!
translator_notes: The declaration kol mitsvotekha emunah ('all your commandments are faithfulness') makes emunah the defining quality of everything God commands. The cry ozreni ('help me!') is one of the psalm's most urgent petitions.

verses.text_hebrew: כִּ֭מְעַט כִּלּ֣וּנִי בָאָ֑רֶץ וַ֝אֲנִ֗י לֹא־עָזַ֥בְתִּי פִקֻּדֶֽיךָ׃
verses.text_kjv: They had almost consumed me upon earth; but I forsook not thy precepts.
verses.rendering:
They nearly destroyed me on earth,
but I did not abandon your precepts.
translator_notes: The word kim'at ('almost, nearly') reveals how close the psalmist came to annihilation. Despite being brought to the brink, the psalmist did not abandon God's precepts.

verses.text_kjv: Quicken me after thy lovingkindness; so shall I keep the testimony of thy mouth.
verses.rendering:
According to your faithful love, revive me,
and I will keep the testimony of your mouth.
translator_notes: The petition kechasdekha chayyeni ('according to your faithful love, revive me') makes God's chesed the basis for the plea for life. The phrase edut pikha ('testimony of your mouth') personalizes the source.

verses.text_kjv: For ever, O LORD, thy word is settled in heaven.
verses.rendering:
Forever, O LORD,
your word stands firm in heaven.
verses.expanded_rendering: The Lamed stanza opens with one of the psalm's most majestic declarations: le'olam YHWH devareka nitsav bashamayim ('forever, O LORD, your word stands firm in heaven'). The verb natsav ('to stand, to be stationed') describes something planted and immovable — God's word is stationed in heaven like a pillar or a sentinel. While the psalmist's body shrivels like a wineskin (v. 83), God's word stands. While persecutors dig pits (v. 85), God's word stands. The contrast between human fragility and divine verbal permanence is the theological backbone of the entire psalm.
translator_notes: Lamed stanza (vv. 89-96). Every verse begins with the Hebrew letter lamed. This stanza is the psalm's cosmological high point — it moves from heaven to earth to the limits of perfection, grounded in the permanence of God's word.

verses.text_hebrew: לְדֹ֣ר וָ֭דֹר אֱמוּנָתֶ֑ךָ כּוֹנַ֥נְתָּ אֶ֝֗רֶץ וַתַּעֲמֹֽד׃
verses.text_kjv: Thy faithfulness is unto all generations: thou hast established the earth, and it abideth.
verses.rendering:
Your faithfulness endures through all generations;
you established the earth, and it stands.
translator_notes: The phrase ledor vador extends God's emunah ('faithfulness') across all human history. The proof is the earth itself: konanta erets vatta'amod ('you established the earth and it stands'). The ground under our feet testifies to the reliability of the God who set it in place.

verses.text_hebrew: לְ֭מִשְׁפָּטֶיךָ עָמְד֣וּ הַיּ֑וֹם כִּ֖י הַכֹּ֣ל עֲבָדֶֽיךָ׃
verses.text_kjv: They continue this day according to thine ordinances: for all are thy servants.
verses.rendering:
By your judgments they stand today,
for all things are your servants.
translator_notes: The entire cosmos stands by God's judgments. The declaration ki hakkol avadekha ('for all things are your servants') is breathtaking: everything that exists serves God. The cosmos is not autonomous but obedient.

verses.text_hebrew: ל֭וּלֵי תוֹרָתְךָ֣ שַׁעֲשֻׁעָ֑י אָ֝֗ז אָבַ֥דְתִּי בְעׇנְיִֽי׃
verses.text_kjv: Unless thy law had been my delights, I should then have perished in mine affliction.
verses.rendering:
If your instruction had not been my delight,
I would have perished in my suffering.
translator_notes: The conditional lulei ('if not') introduces a counterfactual: without Torah as delight, the psalmist would have been destroyed by his affliction. This is not hyperbole — the psalmist credits Torah-delight with saving his life.

verses.text_hebrew: לְ֭עוֹלָם לֹא־אֶשְׁכַּ֥ח פִּקּוּדֶ֗יךָ כִּ֣י בָ֭ם חִיִּיתָֽנִי׃
verses.text_kjv: I will never forget thy precepts: for with them thou hast quickened me.
verses.rendering:
I will never forget your precepts,
for by them you have given me life.
translator_notes: The pledge le'olam lo eshkach ('I will never forget') is absolute. The reason: ki vam chiyyitani ('for by them you have revived me'). The precepts are instruments through which God gives life.

verses.text_hebrew: לְךָ֥ אֲנִי הוֹשִׁיעֵ֑נִי כִּ֖י פִקּוּדֶ֣יךָ דָרָֽשְׁתִּי׃
verses.text_kjv: I am thine, save me; for I have sought thy precepts.
verses.rendering:
I am yours — save me,
for I have sought your precepts.
translator_notes: The declaration lekha ani ('I am yours') is one of the psalm's most intimate statements — a claim of belonging, ownership, covenant identity. Because I belong to you, you are responsible for my rescue.

verses.text_hebrew: לִ֤י קִוּ֣וּ רְ֭שָׁעִים לְאַבְּדֵ֑נִי עֵ֝דֹתֶ֗יךָ אֶתְבּוֹנָֽן׃
verses.text_kjv: The wicked have waited for me to destroy me: but I will consider thy testimonies.
verses.rendering:
The wicked lie in wait to destroy me,
but I consider your testimonies.
translator_notes: The verb qivvu ('they wait, they lie in wait') describes patient, predatory waiting. Against this lethal patience, the psalmist sets his own careful attention: edotekha etbonan ('your testimonies I consider').

verses.text_hebrew: לְכׇל־תִּ֭כְלָה רָאִ֣יתִי קֵ֑ץ רְחָבָ֖ה מִצְוָתְךָ֣ מְאֹֽד׃
verses.text_kjv: I have seen an end of all perfection: but thy commandment is exceeding broad.
verses.rendering:
I have seen a limit to all perfection,
but your commandment is boundlessly wide.
verses.expanded_rendering: The word tikhlah ('perfection, completeness, limit') refers to the highest human achievement — and the psalmist has seen its qets ('end, boundary'). Every human accomplishment has an edge where it runs out. But God's commandment is rechavah me'od ('exceedingly wide, boundlessly broad'). This is perhaps the psalm's single most profound theological statement: human perfection is finite; divine instruction is infinite. No one ever reaches the bottom of God's commandment — there is always more width, more depth, more to explore.
translator_notes: The adjective rechavah ('wide, broad') echoes verse 45's rechavah ('broad place') — the spaciousness that comes from engaging with God's precepts. Here the broadness is attributed to God's commandment itself.

verses.text_hebrew: מָ֣ה אָ֭הַבְתִּי תוֹרָתֶ֑ךָ כׇּל־הַ֝יּ֗וֹם הִ֣יא שִׂיחָתִֽי׃
verses.text_kjv: O how love I thy law! it is my meditation all the day.
verses.rendering:
How I love your instruction!
It is my meditation all day long.
translator_notes: Mem stanza (vv. 97-104). Every verse begins with the Hebrew letter mem. The stanza opens with an exclamation of love: mah ahavti torathekha ('how I love your instruction!'). Torah-meditation is not a scheduled activity but a constant state of mind.

verses.text_hebrew: מֵ֭אֹיְבַי תְּחַכְּמֵ֥נִי מִצְוֺתֶ֑ךָ כִּ֖י לְעוֹלָ֣ם הִיא־לִֽי׃
verses.text_kjv: Thou through thy commandments hast made me wiser than mine enemies: for they are ever with me.
verses.rendering:
Your commandments make me wiser than my enemies,
for they are always with me.
translator_notes: The verb chakam in the Piel (techakkemeni, 'you make me wise') attributes wisdom directly to God's commandments. The comparative me'oyevai ('more than my enemies') says engagement with God's commandments produces wisdom surpassing what opponents possess.

verses.text_hebrew: מִכׇּל־מְ֭לַמְּדַי הִשְׂכַּ֑לְתִּי כִּ֥י עֵ֝דְוֺתֶ֗יךָ שִׂ֣יחָה לִֽי׃
verses.text_kjv: I have more understanding than all my teachers: for thy testimonies are my meditation.
verses.rendering:
I have more insight than all my teachers,
for your testimonies are my meditation.
translator_notes: The claim is not arrogance but a statement about the source of wisdom: teachers taught the psalmist, but God's testimonies give deeper insight (sekhel). The verb sakhal describes practical wisdom — the ability to navigate life skillfully.

verses.text_kjv: I understand more than the ancients, because I keep thy precepts.
verses.rendering:
I understand more than the elders,
for I guard your precepts.
translator_notes: The progression — wiser than enemies (v. 98), more insightful than teachers (v. 99), more understanding than elders (v. 100) — climbs the hierarchy of wisdom. Guarding God's specific instructions produces understanding that exceeds accumulated human experience.

verses.text_hebrew: מִכׇּל־אֹ֣רַח רָ֭ע כָּלִ֣אתִ רַגְלָ֑י לְ֝מַ֗עַן אֶשְׁמֹ֥ר דְּבָרֶֽךָ׃
verses.text_kjv: I have refrained my feet from every evil way, that I might keep thy word.
verses.rendering:
I hold back my feet from every evil path,
in order to keep your word.
translator_notes: The verb kala' ('to restrain, to hold back') describes deliberate self-restraint. Restraint from evil is not an end in itself but a means of preserving faithfulness to God's word.

verses.text_kjv: I have not departed from thy judgments: for thou hast taught me.
verses.rendering:
I have not turned from your judgments,
for you yourself have taught me.
translator_notes: The emphasis on attah ('you') highlights that God is the personal instructor. When God teaches directly, the student does not wander.

verses.text_kjv: How sweet are thy words unto my taste! yea, sweeter than honey to my mouth!
verses.rendering:
How sweet is your promise to my taste —
sweeter than honey to my mouth!
translator_notes: The verb malats ('to be smooth, to be sweet') describes the sensation of God's promise on the palate. The comparison middvash ('sweeter than honey') echoes Psalm 19:10. The psalmist experiences God's word sensorially — it has a taste, and the taste is sweet.

verses.text_hebrew: מִפִּקּוּדֶ֥יךָ אֶתְבּוֹנָ֑ן עַל־כֵּ֝֗ן שָׂנֵ֤אתִי ׀ כׇּל־אֹ֬רַח שָֽׁקֶר׃
verses.text_kjv: Through thy precepts I get understanding: therefore I hate every false way.
verses.rendering:
From your precepts I gain understanding;
therefore I hate every false path.
translator_notes: Understanding gained from God's precepts produces a moral consequence: hatred of every false path. The stronger one's understanding of truth becomes, the stronger the repulsion toward falsehood.

verses.text_kjv: Thy word is a lamp unto my feet, and a light unto my path.
verses.rendering:
Your word is a lamp for my feet
and a light for my path.
verses.expanded_rendering: This is perhaps the most famous verse in the psalm. The image is precise: a ner ('lamp') for the raglai ('my feet') — not a floodlight that illuminates the entire landscape but a small lamp that shows the next step. The parallel or lintivati ('a light for my path') broadens slightly but the emphasis remains on immediate, practical guidance. God's word does not reveal the entire future; it shows where to place the next foot. The metaphor assumes darkness — the psalmist is walking in the dark and needs light. Without God's word, the path is invisible.
translator_notes: Nun stanza (vv. 105-112). Every verse begins with the Hebrew letter nun. The word ner is a small, portable oil lamp — the kind a person would carry at night on a dark road. This is not abstract enlightenment but practical illumination for a real journey.

verses.text_kjv: I have sworn, and I will perform it, that I will keep thy righteous judgments.
verses.rendering:
I have sworn an oath and confirmed it:
to keep your righteous judgments.
translator_notes: The verb nishba ('I have sworn') describes a formal oath — a binding commitment. The verb aqayyemah ('I will confirm') from qum means to stand by the oath, to make it real.

verses.text_kjv: I am afflicted very much: quicken me, O LORD, according unto thy word.
verses.rendering:
I am deeply afflicted;
O LORD, revive me according to your word.
translator_notes: The verb anah ('to be afflicted') with ad me'od ('exceedingly') describes severe suffering. The cry chayyeni kidvarekha is the psalm's recurring lifeline.

verses.text_hebrew: נִדְב֣וֹת פִּ֭י רְצֵה־נָ֣א יְהוָ֑ה וּ֝מִשְׁפָּטֶ֗יךָ לַמְּדֵֽנִי׃
verses.text_kjv: Accept, I beseech thee, the freewill offerings of my mouth, O LORD, and teach me thy judgments.
verses.rendering:
Accept the freewill offerings of my mouth, O LORD,
and teach me your judgments.
translator_notes: The nidvot pi ('freewill offerings of my mouth') are voluntary offerings of praise and prayer — not sacrificial animals but words spoken freely. Worship is the proper posture for receiving instruction.

verses.text_hebrew: נַפְשִׁ֣י בְכַפִּ֣י תָמִ֑יד וְ֝תוֹרָתְךָ֗ לֹ֣א שָׁכָֽחְתִּי׃
verses.text_kjv: My soul is continually in my hand: yet do I not forget thy law.
verses.rendering:
My life is constantly in my hands,
yet I do not forget your instruction.
translator_notes: The phrase nafshi vekhappi tamid ('my life is in my palm continually') is an idiom for living in constant danger. Despite perpetual risk, the psalmist does not forget Torah.

verses.text_hebrew: נָתְנ֬וּ רְשָׁעִ֣ים פַּ֣ח לִ֑י וּ֝מִפִּקּוּדֶ֗יךָ לֹ֣א תָעִֽיתִי׃
verses.text_kjv: The wicked have laid a snare for me: yet I erred not from thy precepts.
verses.rendering:
The wicked set a trap for me,
but I do not stray from your precepts.
translator_notes: The word pach ('trap, snare') describes a concealed device. The traps failed to divert the psalmist from the path.

verses.text_hebrew: נָחַ֣לְתִּי עֵדְוֺתֶ֣יךָ לְעוֹלָ֑ם כִּי־שְׂשׂ֖וֹן לִבִּ֣י הֵֽמָּה׃
verses.text_kjv: Thy testimonies have I taken as an heritage for ever: for they are the rejoicing of my heart.
verses.rendering:
Your testimonies are my inheritance forever,
for they are the joy of my heart.
translator_notes: The verb nachal ('to inherit') treats God's testimonies as the legacy the psalmist has received and will hold forever. The word sason ('joy, exultation') describes the purest happiness.

verses.text_hebrew: נָטִ֣יתִי לִ֭בִּי לַעֲשׂ֥וֹת חֻקֶּ֗יךָ לְעוֹלָ֥ם עֵֽקֶב׃
verses.text_kjv: I have inclined mine heart to perform thy statutes alway, even unto the end.
verses.rendering:
I incline my heart to perform your statutes
forever, to the very end.
translator_notes: The verb natah ('to incline') describes the psalmist deliberately directing his heart toward obedience. In verse 36 he asked God to incline his heart; here he inclines it himself. Both are true — the psalmist acts, and God enables.

verses.text_kjv: I hate vain thoughts: but thy law do I love.
verses.rendering:
I hate the double-minded,
but your instruction I love.
translator_notes: Samekh stanza (vv. 113-120). Every verse begins with the Hebrew letter samekh. The word se'appim is rare — it may mean 'divided ones, double-minded people' (from sa'aph, 'to divide'). The contrast with loving Torah is clear: the psalmist rejects divided loyalty.

verses.text_kjv: Thou art my hiding place and my shield: I hope in thy word.
verses.rendering:
You are my shelter and my shield;
I put my hope in your word.
translator_notes: The words sitri ('my hiding place') and maginni ('my shield') describe God as both passive protection (a place to hide) and active protection (a shield that deflects blows).

verses.text_kjv: Depart from me, ye evildoers: for I will keep the commandments of my God.
verses.rendering:
Depart from me, you evildoers,
for I will guard the commandments of my God.
translator_notes: The imperative suru ('depart') is addressed directly to the mere'im ('evildoers'). The phrase mitsvot Elohai ('commandments of my God') uses the personal possessive — my God — emphasizing the personal relationship that motivates obedience.

verses.text_kjv: Uphold me according unto thy word, that I may live: and let me not be ashamed of my hope.
verses.rendering:
Sustain me according to your promise, that I may live,
and do not let me be ashamed of my hope.
translator_notes: The verb samakh ('to support, to sustain') asks God to hold the psalmist up. The plea al tevisheni misivri ('do not let me be ashamed of my hope') reveals the deepest fear: that hoping in God might prove to be a mistake.

verses.text_kjv: Hold thou me up, and I shall be safe: and I will have respect unto thy statutes continually.
verses.rendering:
Support me, and I will be saved;
I will gaze on your statutes continually.
translator_notes: The verb sa'ad ('to support, to sustain') is parallel to samakh in verse 116. Safety and sustained attention to Torah go together.

verses.text_kjv: Thou hast trodden down all them that err from thy statutes: for their deceit is falsehood.
verses.rendering:
You reject all who stray from your statutes,
for their cunning is a lie.
translator_notes: The verb salah ('to reject, to make light of') describes God dismissing those who wander from his statutes. Their tarmit ('deceit, cunning') is sheqer ('falsehood') — their cleverness is built on nothing.

verses.text_hebrew: סִגִּ֗ים הִשְׁבַּ֥תָּ כׇל־רִשְׁעֵי־אֶ֑רֶץ לָ֝כֵ֗ן אָהַ֥בְתִּי עֵדֹתֶֽיךָ׃
verses.text_kjv: Thou puttest away all the wicked of the earth like dross: therefore I love thy testimonies.
verses.rendering:
You remove all the wicked of the earth like dross;
therefore I love your testimonies.
translator_notes: The word sigim ('dross, slag') is the waste material from metal refining. God puts away the wicked like impurities being skimmed off molten metal. Because God purifies, the psalmist loves the testimonies that define the standard.

verses.text_kjv: My flesh trembleth for fear of thee; and I am afraid of thy judgments.
verses.rendering:
My flesh shudders in awe of you,
and I stand in fear of your judgments.
translator_notes: The verb samar ('to bristle, to shudder') describes a physical reaction — the psalmist's skin prickles and hair stands up in response to God's presence. This is not terror but awe — the bodily response to encountering something overwhelming.

verses.text_kjv: I have done judgment and justice: leave me not to mine oppressors.
verses.rendering:
I have done what is just and right;
do not abandon me to my oppressors.
translator_notes: Ayin stanza (vv. 121-128). Every verse begins with the Hebrew letter ayin. The psalmist claims moral integrity as the basis for petition: do not leave me to my oppressors.

verses.text_kjv: Be surety for thy servant for good: let not the proud oppress me.
verses.rendering:
Be your servant's guarantor for good;
do not let the arrogant oppress me.
translator_notes: The verb arav ('to pledge, to be surety') asks God to stand as guarantor — to co-sign the psalmist's future. This is financial language applied to the covenant relationship.

verses.text_kjv: Mine eyes fail for thy salvation, and for the word of thy righteousness.
verses.rendering:
My eyes fail with watching for your salvation
and for your righteous promise.
translator_notes: As in verse 82, the eyes fail from straining to see deliverance. Both God's saving act and God's righteous word are delayed, and the waiting exhausts the psalmist's sight.

verses.text_kjv: Deal with thy servant according unto thy mercy, and teach me thy statutes.
verses.rendering:
Deal with your servant according to your faithful love,
and teach me your statutes.
translator_notes: The petition asks God to let chesed be the standard by which he treats the psalmist — not strict justice but covenant loyalty.

verses.text_kjv: I am thy servant; give me understanding, that I may know thy testimonies.
verses.rendering:
I am your servant; give me understanding,
that I may know your testimonies.
translator_notes: The declaration avdekha ani ('your servant I am') reaffirms the psalmist's identity. Knowledge of God's testimonies comes only through divinely given understanding.

verses.text_kjv: It is time for thee, LORD, to work: for they have made void thy law.
verses.rendering:
It is time for the LORD to act,
for they have broken your instruction.
translator_notes: The phrase et la'asot laYHWH ('it is time for the LORD to act') is an urgent call for divine intervention. The verb parar ('to break, to annul') in the Hiphil describes deliberate shattering of Torah.

verses.text_kjv: Therefore I love thy commandments above gold; yea, above fine gold.
verses.rendering:
Therefore I love your commandments
more than gold, more than fine gold.
translator_notes: Because others break Torah, the psalmist loves it more. The comparison uses two words for gold — zahav and paz (the purest, most refined gold). Even the finest gold falls short.

verses.text_hebrew: עַל־כֵּ֤ן ׀ כׇּל־פִּקּ֣וּדֵי כֹ֭ל יִשָּׁ֑רְתִּי כׇּל־אֹ֖רַח שֶׁ֣קֶר שָׂנֵֽאתִי׃
verses.text_kjv: Therefore I esteem all thy precepts concerning all things to be right; and I hate every false way.
verses.rendering:
Therefore I consider all your precepts — every one of them — right,
and I hate every false path.
translator_notes: The emphatic repetition kol piqqudei khol ('all precepts, all of them') insists on comprehensiveness. The stanza closes with hatred of falsehood that matches verse 104.

verses.text_kjv: Thy testimonies are wonderful: therefore doth my soul keep them.
verses.rendering:
Your testimonies are wonderful;
therefore my soul guards them.
translator_notes: Pe stanza (vv. 129-136). Every verse begins with the Hebrew letter pe. The word pela'ot ('wonderful, beyond comprehension') describes the testimonies as exceeding normal understanding. The response to wonder is guarding.

verses.text_kjv: The entrance of thy words giveth light; it giveth understanding unto the simple.
verses.rendering:
The unfolding of your words gives light;
it gives understanding to the simple.
verses.expanded_rendering: The word petach ('opening, entrance, unfolding') from patach ('to open') describes what happens when God's words are opened up — they emit light (ya'ir). The image is of a door or scroll being opened and light pouring out from within. The beneficiaries are the petayim ('simple ones, naive') — those who lack sophistication but are open to learning. God's words do not require pre-existing brilliance to illuminate; they create understanding in those who have none.
translator_notes: The word petayim ('simple, naive') from patah describes someone open and impressionable — capable of being shaped for good or ill. God's words provide the shaping that turns simplicity into understanding.

verses.text_kjv: I opened my mouth, and panted: for I longed for thy commandments.
verses.rendering:
I open my mouth and pant,
for I long for your commandments.
translator_notes: The verb pa'ar ('to open wide') and sha'aph ('to gasp, to pant') together picture someone gasping for air. The psalmist pants for God's commandments the way a thirsty animal pants for water.

verses.text_kjv: Look thou upon me, and be merciful unto me, as thou usest to do unto those that love thy name.
verses.rendering:
Turn to me and be gracious to me,
as is your way with those who love your name.
translator_notes: The phrase kemishpat le'ohavei shemekha ('according to the custom for those who love your name') appeals to precedent: God has a pattern of being gracious to his lovers, and the psalmist asks to be included.

verses.text_kjv: Order my steps in thy word: and let not any iniquity have dominion over me.
verses.rendering:
Steady my steps by your promise,
and let no iniquity rule over me.
translator_notes: The verb kun ('to establish, to make firm') asks God to make the psalmist's steps stable. The verb shalat ('to rule, to have dominion') pictures sin as a potential ruler — the psalmist asks God to prevent sin from exercising sovereignty over his life.

verses.text_kjv: Deliver me from the oppression of man: so will I keep thy precepts.
verses.rendering:
Ransom me from human oppression,
and I will keep your precepts.
translator_notes: The verb padah ('to ransom, to redeem') asks God to pay the price to free the psalmist from oppression. Obedience is the fruit of liberation.

verses.text_kjv: Make thy face to shine upon thy servant; and teach me thy statutes.
verses.rendering:
Make your face shine upon your servant,
and teach me your statutes.
translator_notes: The petition panekha ha'er ('make your face shine') echoes the Aaronic blessing of Numbers 6:25. The shining face of God represents favor, attention, and warmth.

verses.text_hebrew: פַּלְגֵי־מַ֭יִם יָרְד֣וּ עֵינָ֑י עַ֝֗ל לֹא־שָׁמְר֥וּ תוֹרָתֶֽךָ׃
