verses.text_kjv: Rivers of waters run down mine eyes, because they keep not thy law.
verses.rendering:
Streams of water flow from my eyes
because they do not keep your instruction.
verses.expanded_rendering: The phrase palgei mayim ('channels of water, streams') describes not mere tears but rivers — the psalmist's eyes pour out water in continuous streams. The cause is not personal suffering but others' failure: al lo shameru toratekha ('because they do not keep your instruction'). The psalmist weeps for the world's rejection of Torah. This is grief born of love — the psalmist loves God's instruction so deeply that watching others abandon it produces a physical outpouring of sorrow.
translator_notes: The word palgei (from peleg, 'channel, stream') describes permanent water channels, not temporary rivulets. The psalmist's weeping is sustained, not episodic.

verses.text_kjv: Righteous art thou, O LORD, and upright are thy judgments.
verses.rendering:
You are righteous, O LORD,
and your judgments are upright.
translator_notes: Tsade stanza (vv. 137-144). Every verse begins with the Hebrew letter tsade. The stanza opens with a declaration of God's character: tsaddiq attah YHWH. God's judgments are yashar ('upright, straight, level') — not crooked or biased.

verses.text_kjv: Thy testimonies that thou hast commanded are righteous and very faithful.
verses.rendering:
You have commanded your testimonies in righteousness
and in great faithfulness.
translator_notes: God's testimonies are characterized by tsedeq ('righteousness') and emunah ('faithfulness') — righteous in content and faithful in reliability.

verses.text_kjv: My zeal hath consumed me, because mine enemies have forgotten thy words.
verses.rendering:
My zeal consumes me,
because my enemies have forgotten your words.
translator_notes: The verb tsamat ('to destroy, to consume') describes the psalmist being consumed by his own qin'ah ('zeal'). The cause is that his enemies have forgotten God's words — this is covenant grief, not self-pity.

verses.text_kjv: Thy word is very pure: therefore thy servant loveth it.
verses.rendering:
Your promise is thoroughly refined,
and your servant loves it.
translator_notes: The adjective tserufah ('refined, purified') from tsaraph ('to smelt, to refine') describes God's promise as having passed through fire — all impurities burned away. This is metallurgy applied to speech.

verses.text_hebrew: צָעִ֣יר אָנֹכִ֣י וְנִבְזֶ֑ה פִּ֝קֻּדֶ֗יךָ לֹ֣א שָׁכָֽחְתִּי׃
verses.text_kjv: I am small and despised: yet do not I forget thy precepts.
verses.rendering:
I am small and despised,
but I do not forget your precepts.
translator_notes: The psalmist describes himself as tsa'ir ('small, insignificant') and nivzeh ('despised'). Faithfulness to God's word is not a function of social standing.

verses.text_kjv: Thy righteousness is an everlasting righteousness, and thy law is the truth.
verses.rendering:
Your righteousness is righteousness forever,
and your instruction is truth.
translator_notes: God's righteousness does not expire or diminish. And vetoratekha emet ('your instruction is truth') equates Torah with truth itself. Two of the psalm's most absolute claims.

verses.text_kjv: Trouble and anguish have taken hold on me: yet thy commandments are my delights.
verses.rendering:
Trouble and distress have found me,
but your commandments are my delight.
translator_notes: The words tsar ('trouble, narrowness') and matsoq ('anguish, straits') describe being hemmed in — the opposite of the spaciousness of verse 45. Delight persists even when circumstances squeeze.

verses.text_kjv: The righteousness of thy testimonies is everlasting: give me understanding, and I shall live.
verses.rendering:
Your testimonies are righteous forever;
give me understanding, and I will live.
translator_notes: Eternal truth requires ongoing personal illumination. The testimonies do not change, but the psalmist must continually receive new capacity to understand them.

verses.text_hebrew: קָרָ֣אתִי בְכׇל־לֵ֭ב עֲנֵ֥נִי יְהוָ֗ה חֻקֶּ֥יךָ אֶצֹּֽרָה׃
verses.text_kjv: I cried with my whole heart; hear me, O LORD: I will keep thy statutes.
verses.rendering:
I cry out with my whole heart — answer me, O LORD!
I will guard your statutes.
translator_notes: Qoph stanza (vv. 145-152). Every verse begins with the Hebrew letter qoph. The stanza is marked by urgent crying — the psalmist calls, rises early, stays up late, all in desperate petition.

verses.text_kjv: I cried unto thee; save me, and I shall keep thy testimonies.
verses.rendering:
I call to you — save me,
and I will keep your testimonies.
translator_notes: The cry hoshi'eni ('save me') is direct and unadorned. Rescue frees the psalmist for obedience.

verses.text_kjv: I prevented the dawning of the morning, and cried: I hoped in thy word.
verses.rendering:
I rise before dawn and cry for help;
I put my hope in your word.
translator_notes: The verb qiddam ('to precede, to come before') with banneshef ('in the twilight/dawn') means the psalmist gets up before the sun. The predawn darkness is filled with crying and hope.

verses.text_kjv: Mine eyes prevent the night watches, that I might meditate in thy word.
verses.rendering:
My eyes are open before the night watches,
that I may meditate on your promise.
translator_notes: The ashmurot ('night watches') are the divisions of the night. The psalmist's eyes are already open before each watch begins — he stays awake to meditate on God's promise.

verses.text_hebrew: קוֹלִ֣י שִׁ֭מְעָה כְחַסְדֶּ֑ךָ יְ֝הוָ֗ה כְּמִשְׁפָּטֶ֥ךָ חַיֵּֽנִי׃
verses.text_kjv: Hear my voice according unto thy lovingkindness: O LORD, quicken me according to thy judgment.
verses.rendering:
Hear my voice according to your faithful love;
O LORD, revive me according to your judgment.
translator_notes: The petition combines two appeals: hear me according to chesed and revive me according to mishpat. Both God's love and God's justice point toward life.

verses.text_kjv: They draw nigh that follow after mischief: they are far from thy law.
verses.rendering:
Those who pursue evil schemes draw near;
they are far from your instruction.
translator_notes: The spatial contrast is vivid: the pursuers of zimmah ('evil schemes') draw near to the psalmist but are far from God's instruction. Physical proximity to the wicked corresponds to their spiritual distance from Torah.

verses.text_kjv: Thou art near, O LORD; and all thy commandments are truth.
verses.rendering:
You are near, O LORD,
and all your commandments are truth.
translator_notes: Against the nearness of the wicked (v. 150), the psalmist affirms: qarov attah YHWH ('you are near'). God is closer than the enemies. And all God's commandments are emet ('truth').

verses.text_hebrew: קֶ֗דֶם יָ֭דַעְתִּי מֵעֵדֹתֶ֑יךָ כִּ֖י לְעוֹלָ֣ם יְסַדְתָּֽם׃
verses.text_kjv: Concerning thy testimonies, I have known of old that thou hast founded them for ever.
verses.rendering:
Long ago I learned from your testimonies
that you established them forever.
translator_notes: The word qedem ('long ago') indicates the psalmist has known this for a long time. The verb yasad ('to found, to establish') pictures the testimonies as having an unshakable foundation — grounded for eternity.

verses.text_kjv: Consider mine affliction, and deliver me: for I do not forget thy law.
verses.rendering:
See my suffering and rescue me,
for I have not forgotten your instruction.
translator_notes: Resh stanza (vv. 153-160). Every verse begins with the Hebrew letter resh. The stanza opens with a plea to be seen: re'eh onyi ('see my suffering'). The verb chalats ('to draw out, to rescue') asks God to pull the psalmist out of danger.

verses.text_kjv: Plead my cause, and deliver me: quicken me according to thy word.
verses.rendering:
Plead my case and redeem me;
revive me according to your promise.
translator_notes: The verb riv ('to contend, to plead a case') asks God to serve as legal advocate. The verb ga'al ('to redeem, to act as kinsman-redeemer') asks God to buy the psalmist back. Both are legal-relational terms: God as lawyer and God as redeemer.

verses.text_hebrew: רָח֣וֹק מֵרְשָׁעִ֣ים יְשׁוּעָ֑ה כִּי־חֻ֝קֶּ֗יךָ לֹ֣א דָרָֽשׁוּ׃
verses.text_kjv: Salvation is far from the wicked: for they seek not thy statutes.
verses.rendering:
Salvation is far from the wicked,
for they do not seek your statutes.
translator_notes: Those who refuse to seek God's decrees cut themselves off from salvation. The logic is causal: no seeking, no finding.

verses.text_kjv: Great are thy tender mercies, O LORD: quicken me according to thy judgments.
verses.rendering:
Your mercies are great, O LORD;
revive me according to your judgments.
translator_notes: God's rachamim ('mercies, compassion') are rabbim ('many, great'). God's judgments point toward life, not death.

verses.text_hebrew: רַבִּ֣ים רֹדְפַ֣י וְצָרָ֑י מֵ֝עֵדְוֺתֶ֗יךָ לֹ֣א נָטִֽיתִי׃
verses.text_kjv: Many are my persecutors and mine enemies; yet do I not decline from thy testimonies.
verses.rendering:
My persecutors and enemies are many,
but I do not turn from your testimonies.
translator_notes: Despite being outnumbered, the psalmist has not turned aside from God's testimonies.

verses.text_hebrew: רָאִ֣יתִי בֹגְדִ֣ים וָאֶתְקוֹטָ֑טָה אֲשֶׁ֥ר אִ֝מְרָתְךָ֗ לֹ֣א שָׁמָֽרוּ׃
verses.text_kjv: I beheld the transgressors, and was grieved; because they kept not thy word.
verses.rendering:
I look at the faithless and feel disgust,
because they do not keep your promise.
translator_notes: The word bogedim ('faithless, treacherous') describes covenant breakers. The verb qut in the Hitpolel describes a visceral reaction of loathing.

verses.text_hebrew: רְ֭אֵה כִּי־פִקּוּדֶ֣יךָ אָהָ֑בְתִּי יְ֝הוָ֗ה כְּחַסְדְּךָ֥ חַיֵּֽנִי׃
verses.text_kjv: Consider how I love thy precepts: quicken me, O LORD, according to thy lovingkindness.
verses.rendering:
See how I love your precepts!
O LORD, revive me according to your faithful love.
translator_notes: The imperative re'eh asks God to look at the psalmist's love. Human love for God's precepts and divine faithful love together form the ground of the plea for revival.

verses.text_hebrew: רֹ֣אשׁ דְּבָרְךָ֣ אֱמֶ֑ת וּלְעוֹלָ֗ם כׇּל־מִשְׁפַּ֥ט צִדְקֶֽךָ׃
verses.text_kjv: Thy word is true from the beginning: and every one of thy righteous judgments endureth for ever.
verses.rendering:
The sum of your word is truth,
and every one of your righteous judgments endures forever.
verses.expanded_rendering: The word rosh ('head, beginning, sum') can mean either 'from the beginning' or 'the totality/sum.' Both readings are valid: God's word has been truth from its very first utterance, and the totality of God's word adds up to truth. The second line makes the claim comprehensive and eternal: not just some but every judgment, and not just for now but forever. This verse functions as a summary statement for the entire psalm: everything God has said is true, and every ruling God has made is permanently righteous.
translator_notes: The word rosh is debated: 'the beginning of your word is truth' (temporal) or 'the sum of your word is truth' (comprehensive). The rendering 'the sum of your word' captures the comprehensive sense.

verses.text_hebrew: שָׂרִ֣ים רְדָפ֣וּנִי חִנָּ֑ם וּ֝מִדְּבָרְךָ֗ פָּחַ֥ד לִבִּֽי׃
verses.text_kjv: Princes have persecuted me without a cause: but my heart standeth in awe of thy word.
verses.rendering:
Rulers persecute me without cause,
but my heart stands in awe of your word.
translator_notes: Shin stanza (vv. 161-168). Every verse begins with the Hebrew letter shin. The sarim from verse 23 return. The verb pachad ('to tremble, to be in awe') describes the heart's response to God's word as more powerful than its response to human threats.

verses.text_hebrew: שָׂ֣שׂ אָ֭נֹכִי עַל־אִמְרָתֶ֑ךָ כְּ֝מוֹצֵ֗א שָׁלָ֥ל רָֽב׃
verses.text_kjv: I rejoice at thy word, as one that findeth great spoil.
verses.rendering:
I rejoice over your promise
like one who finds great treasure.
translator_notes: The comparison kemotse shalal rav ('like one who finds great plunder') evokes the elation of discovering vast, unexpected treasure. God's promise is windfall wealth.

verses.text_kjv: I hate and abhor lying: but thy law do I love.
verses.rendering:
I hate and despise falsehood,
but your instruction I love.
translator_notes: Two verbs of revulsion (sane'ti and va'ata'evah) are stacked against one verb of love. The psalmist despises deception with double intensity and loves Torah with simple, direct affection.

verses.text_hebrew: שֶׁ֣בַע בַּ֭יּוֹם הִלַּלְתִּ֑יךָ עַ֝֗ל מִשְׁפְּטֵ֥י צִדְקֶֽךָ׃
verses.text_kjv: Seven times a day do I praise thee because of thy righteous judgments.
verses.rendering:
Seven times a day I praise you
for your righteous judgments.
translator_notes: The number sheva ('seven') may be literal or may represent completeness — the psalmist praises God a full, complete number of times throughout the day.

verses.text_hebrew: שָׁל֣וֹם רָ֭ב לְאֹהֲבֵ֣י תוֹרָתֶ֑ךָ וְאֵֽין־לָ֥מוֹ מִכְשֽׁוֹל׃
verses.text_kjv: Great peace have they which love thy law: and nothing shall offend them.
verses.rendering:
Great peace belongs to those who love your instruction,
and nothing causes them to stumble.
verses.expanded_rendering: The phrase shalom rav ('great peace, abundant peace') describes not merely the absence of conflict but comprehensive well-being — wholeness, completeness, flourishing. This shalom belongs to those who love God's instruction. The second line — ve'ein lamo mikhshol ('and there is no stumbling block for them') — describes a path without obstacles. Those who love Torah are not exempt from suffering (the entire psalm testifies to the psalmist's suffering), but they are exempt from the fundamental stumbling that comes from walking without direction. The peace is not circumstantial but existential — it is the deep stability of someone whose life is oriented by something true.
translator_notes: The word shalom from shalem ('to be complete, to be whole') is one of the richest words in Hebrew. Here modified by rav ('great'), it describes overflowing peace from loving God's instruction. The word mikhshol ('stumbling block') comes from kashal ('to stumble').

verses.text_kjv: LORD, I have hoped for thy salvation, and done thy commandments.
verses.rendering:
I hope for your salvation, O LORD,
and I do your commandments.
translator_notes: Hope and obedience coexist. The psalmist does not wait passively for God to act but obeys actively while waiting.

verses.text_kjv: My soul hath kept thy testimonies; and I love them exceedingly.
verses.rendering:
My soul keeps your testimonies,
and I love them deeply.
translator_notes: Soul-keeping and deep love together describe the most complete devotion possible.

verses.text_hebrew: שָׁ֭מַרְתִּי פִקּוּדֶ֣יךָ וְעֵדֹתֶ֑יךָ כִּ֖י כׇל־דְּרָכַ֣י נֶגְדֶּֽךָ׃
verses.text_kjv: I have kept thy precepts and thy testimonies: for all my ways are before thee.
verses.rendering:
I keep your precepts and your testimonies,
for all my ways are before you.
translator_notes: The motivating clause ki khol derakhay negdekha ('for all my ways are before you') reveals awareness that God sees everything. Nothing is hidden, and this awareness motivates faithfulness.

verses.text_hebrew: תִּקְרַ֤ב רִנָּתִ֣י לְפָנֶ֣יךָ יְהוָ֑ה כִּדְבָרְךָ֥ הֲבִינֵֽנִי׃
verses.text_kjv: Let my cry come near before thee, O LORD: give me understanding according to thy word.
verses.rendering:
Let my cry come before you, O LORD;
give me understanding according to your word.
translator_notes: Tav stanza (vv. 169-176). Every verse begins with the Hebrew letter tav, the last letter of the Hebrew alphabet. The psalm's final stanza is a cascade of petitions — the psalmist has reached the end of the alphabet and pours out his final requests.

verses.text_kjv: Let my supplication come before thee: deliver me according to thy word.
verses.rendering:
Let my plea come before you;
deliver me according to your promise.
translator_notes: The word techinnati ('my supplication, my plea for grace') from chanan is a request for unmerited favor. The petition hatssileni ('deliver me') asks God to snatch the psalmist out of danger.

verses.text_hebrew: תַּבַּ֣עְנָה שְׂפָתַ֣י תְּהִלָּ֑ה כִּ֖י תְלַמְּדֵ֣נִי חֻקֶּֽיךָ׃
verses.text_kjv: My lips shall utter praise, when thou hast taught me thy statutes.
verses.rendering:
My lips will pour out praise,
for you teach me your statutes.
translator_notes: The verb nava ('to bubble up, to pour forth') describes praise flowing from the lips like water from a spring. Teaching produces praise.

verses.text_hebrew: תַּ֣עַן לְ֭שׁוֹנִי אִמְרָתֶ֑ךָ כִּ֖י כׇל־מִצְוֺתֶ֣יךָ צֶּֽדֶק׃
verses.text_kjv: My tongue shall speak of thy word: for all thy commandments are righteousness.
verses.rendering:
My tongue will sing of your promise,
for all your commandments are righteous.
translator_notes: The verb anah ('to answer, to sing, to respond') describes the tongue responding to God's promise — the tongue sings because it has been addressed.

verses.text_kjv: Let thine hand help me; for I have chosen thy precepts.
verses.rendering:
Let your hand be ready to help me,
for I have chosen your precepts.
translator_notes: The petition asks for God's active intervention — his hand, not just his attention. The basis is choice: ki piqqudekha bacharti ('for I have chosen your precepts').

verses.text_kjv: I have longed for thy salvation, O LORD; and thy law is my delight.
verses.rendering:
I long for your salvation, O LORD,
and your instruction is my delight.
translator_notes: The psalmist's longing for God's salvation is undiminished at the psalm's end. And toratekha sha'ashu'ai ('your instruction is my delight') repeats the central claim one final time.

verses.text_kjv: Let my soul live, and it shall praise thee; and let thy judgments help me.
verses.rendering:
Let my soul live, and it will praise you,
and let your judgments help me.
translator_notes: The petition techi nafshi ('let my soul live') is the psalm's final cry for life. The purpose of life is praise: utehallelekka. Life is not an end in itself but the means by which praise continues.

verses.text_hebrew: תָּעִ֗יתִי כְּשֶׂ֣ה אֹ֭בֵד בַּקֵּ֣שׁ עַבְדֶּ֑ךָ כִּ֥י מִ֝צְוֺתֶ֗יךָ לֹ֣א שָׁכָֽחְתִּי׃
verses.text_kjv: I have gone astray like a lost sheep; seek thy servant; for I do not forget thy commandments.
verses.expanded_rendering: The psalm's final verse is startling: after 176 verses of devotion, meditation, delight, and commitment, the psalmist confesses ta'iti keseh oved ('I have wandered like a lost sheep'). The verb ta'ah is the same verb used throughout the psalm for straying from God's path. The comparison to a seh oved ('lost sheep') is an image of helplessness — a sheep cannot find its own way home. The psalmist who has spent the entire psalm declaring his love for God's instruction ends by admitting that he is lost and needs to be found. The petition baqqesh avdekha ('seek your servant') reverses the psalm's dominant verb: throughout the psalm, the psalmist seeks God and his word; now he asks God to seek him. The final clause — ki mitsvotekha lo shakhachti ('for I have not forgotten your commandments') — is not a boast but a signal: I may be lost, but I still remember. I have wandered, but I have not abandoned. Come find me, because the thread that connects us — your commandments in my memory — has not been broken. This is the psalm's most profound admission: perfect obedience was never the point. The point was never letting go of the thread.
translator_notes: This final verse reframes the entire psalm. The psalmist is not a paragon of perfection but a wanderer who loves what he cannot perfectly keep. The tension between 'I have not forgotten your commandments' and 'I have wandered like a lost sheep' is the tension of the entire human relationship with God's word: we know it, we love it, we stray from it, and we need to be found.

extended_references.label: Torah-Psalm Introduction
extended_references.note: Psalm 119 is the culmination of the Torah-psalm tradition that begins with Psalm 1 (the blessed person delights in the LORD's instruction) and Psalm 19 (the instruction of the LORD is complete). What those psalms state briefly, Psalm 119 elaborates across 176 verses.

extended_references.label: Deuteronomic Vocabulary
extended_references.note: The eight torah-words in Psalm 119 draw heavily on Deuteronomy's vocabulary for covenant obligation. Moses commands Israel to keep statutes, commandments, judgments, and testimonies — the same categories the psalmist meditates on.

extended_references.label: Word as Light
extended_references.note: The image of God's word as light (vv. 105, 130) connects to the broader biblical metaphor of divine instruction as illumination in a dark world.

extended_references.label: Lost Sheep
extended_references.note: The psalm's closing image of a lost sheep anticipates Isaiah's confession ('all of us like sheep have gone astray') and Ezekiel's promise that God himself will seek his sheep.
